lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing outsourced, specification-driven architectural millwork production, including custom molding, trim, ornamental woodwork, stairwork, shutters, and closely related made-to-order components for external builders, dealers, architects, and contractors.
lens.excluded_activities: Standard wood flooring and stock millwork sold as products without an outsourced-service relationship, on-site construction fabrication, wood windows and doors, cut stock, captive shops, pure distributors, software vendors, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Eligible firms earn repeat B2B revenue by engineering and fabricating customer- or project-specific millwork, commonly through quotes, purchase orders, builder programs, and project releases; commodity or branded flooring sales are excluded for coherence.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines custom architectural millwork with standardized wood flooring and stock products. The lens is narrowed to recurring specification-driven millwork fabrication because flooring's product, retail, and consumer-demand economics are too different for a coherent single screen; the narrowing is based on business-model coherence, not attractiveness.

a.bridge: BLS occupation counts cover broader other-wood-product manufacturing, while commercial CAD/CAM and automation sources describe custom cabinetry and millwork rather than a wage-weighted NAICS 321918 task sample. These are mapped to design translation, estimating, CNC programming, nesting, inspection, scheduling, material handling, and customer-document workflows. The bridge may overstate exposure in craft-heavy shops and understate it in digitally mature CNC operations.
a.rationale: Custom millwork combines physical machining and handling with unusually information-intensive work: interpreting drawings, takeoffs, estimating, submittals, shop drawings, CNC programming, nesting, revisions, scheduling, and quality records. CAD/CAM already connects designs to CNC machinery, and vendors offer vision-guided sorting and robotic tending. AI can extend these workflows, but irregular geometries, finishing, assembly, installation interfaces, and craftsmanship keep substantial labor outside direct substitution.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study across custom architectural millwork firms measuring hours in sales takeoff, engineering, submittals, CNC programming, machining, sanding, finishing, assembly, inspection, material movement, project management, and rework, segmented by current CAD/CAM maturity.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task study isolates custom millwork from flooring.
Automation vendors describe technical capability rather than measured labor substitution.
The supplied compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis.

rho.rationale: Document, takeoff, quoting, submittal, scheduling, and programming assistance can be implemented within five years, and existing CAD/CAM infrastructure provides an integration path. Shop-floor vision and robotics face irregular parts, frequent changeovers, finish variation, legacy machinery, and safety constraints. Firms with disciplined product data will realize much more than craft shops with bespoke drawings and tribal knowledge.
rho.change_evidence: Before/after deployment evidence from 15-20 LMM millwork firms reporting quote and submittal cycle time, engineering hours, CNC-programming time, yield, rework, inspection escapes, setup time, machine utilization, labor hours, and adoption by plant and workflow.
caveats: No representative five-year implementation cohort was found.
AI adoption evidence covers all US businesses rather than millwork firms.
A 2024 recordable injury rate of 4.0 per 100 workers increases the burden of safe shop-floor deployment.

e.rationale: The narrowed lens excludes standardized flooring and stock products but retains custom molding, stairwork, ornamental woodwork, and project-specific components that builders and contractors repeatedly outsource. The Census definition confirms both the diverse product set and the exclusion of on-site work. Builders FirstSource describes custom millwork and long-term builder relationships, supporting a meaningful service-like subset, but the eligible share is not measured.
e.change_evidence: Classify the supplied 285-firm LMM estimate by revenue from custom/project millwork, repeat builder programs, engineering and submittals, standard flooring, stock moldings, retail sales, installation, and distribution using websites, contracts, and management interviews.
caveats: NAICS does not publish firm counts by custom versus stock revenue model.
Project-specific products are not necessarily recurring outsourced services.
The supplied firm count is a margin-bridged estimate based on 2022 receipts and a January 2026 sector margin.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or ownership-transfer plans among US small-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers among eligible LMM custom millwork firms. Mature craft-business ownership could lift propensity toward the 17% observed for owners 55 and older, while failed plans, key-person dependence, and nonqualifying family gifts reduce realized transferable events.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports 14% of working owners planning a sale, IPO, or ownership transfer within five years and 17% among owners age 55 or older. A 17% base reflects the likely mature, founder-led profile of fragmented custom millwork firms, while the range recognizes that owner relationships and design knowledge can impair transferability.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age, key-person, and succession interviews for eligible firms plus five years of closed control transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, AWI contacts, brokers, and state filings, excluding internal reorganizations and non-control events.
caveats: Stated plans are not completed transactions.
The Gallup sample spans industries and smaller businesses.
Gifts and rare IPOs in the source category may not qualify.

q.rationale: Custom millwork competes on design execution, local knowledge, service, quality, price, and on-time delivery. Benefits from faster takeoffs, fewer drawing errors, lower rework, improved nesting, and more reliable schedules are not fully transparent to buyers and can be retained through fixed project quotes. Repeat builders still rebid work and use multiple suppliers, so visible labor savings and procurement gains will be shared over renewals.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm bid and contract analysis covering fixed price versus cost-plus terms, change-order ownership, open-book costing, price-reset cadence, competitive bid frequency, customer concentration, gross-margin persistence, and warranty or back-charge changes after digital improvements.
caveats: Builders FirstSource is a much larger customer and competitor than target firms.
No study directly observes retention of AI-created benefit in custom millwork.
Capture is lower in standardized moldings and higher in complex architectural packages.

d5.bridge: CBO housing starts, Harvard remodeling spending, and broad flooring and building-products disclosures proxy for constant-price, constant-quality demand for custom US millwork. Remodeling figures are nominal and flooring is excluded from the narrowed lens, which can overstate real millwork quantity; custom commercial work and restoration could make the lens less housing-sensitive than the proxy.
d5.rationale: Custom millwork demand is tied to new construction, remodeling, and project turnover. CBO projected strong average housing starts through 2029, while Harvard's 2026 indicator expected remodeling growth to slow sharply in early 2027. Flooring and building-product disclosures confirm intense sensitivity to housing, rates, credit, and remodeling. A near-flat-to-modest-growth base balances medium-term housing support against current softness and substitution toward simpler or non-wood finishes.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-dollar and unit forecasts from AWI, Dodge, ConstructConnect, Harvard JCHS, and end-market data providers segmented into custom residential millwork, commercial architectural woodwork, stair parts, moldings, and shutters, with backlog and bid-volume data from eligible firms.
caveats: Housing starts are an imperfect proxy for repair, remodel, and commercial custom millwork.
The Harvard indicator is nominal and only four quarters forward.
Flooring market data mainly illuminate cyclicality and substitution, not the narrowed service quantity.

o.rationale: Custom millwork remains a physical, project-specific output requiring accountable interpretation of drawings, material selection, machining, finishing, quality control, delivery, and coordination. Software can automate much of the information flow but cannot supply the installed-quality component by itself. Modular construction, customer insourcing, imports, CNC job shops, or substitution to stock and non-wood products can remove some demand from operators of the current kind.
o.change_evidence: Builder, architect, general-contractor, dealer, and casework-shop interviews on five-year plans for design self-service, direct imports, stock-product substitution, modularization, in-house CNC, and procurement from automated platform-based fabricators.
caveats: The excluded flooring segment has different substitution dynamics.
Some digital fabrication platforms could consolidate rather than eliminate the accountable operator.
No direct customer survey measures future insourcing or self-service for custom millwork.

narrative.executive_view: Custom architectural millwork has substantial information-heavy labor around takeoffs, drawings, submittals, quoting, programming, and project coordination, alongside physical machining and finishing. That combination creates meaningful AI opportunity. The NAICS code is heterogeneous, however, so the coherent lens excludes standardized flooring and stock products and focuses on recurring specification-driven fabrication.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can interpret plans, draft takeoffs, generate quotes and submittals, assist CNC programming and nesting, schedule jobs, detect finish or dimensional defects, and support maintenance and customer communication. Existing CAD/CAM provides a foundation. Irregular geometry, material variation, sanding, finishing, assembly, handling, and project exceptions keep humans and specialized equipment central.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed-price custom projects can retain gains from faster engineering, better yield, fewer errors, and lower rework because customers buy a finished specification and schedule rather than disclosed labor hours. Repeat builder procurement and rebidding eventually share visible savings. Complexity, service reliability, and execution quality are the main defenses against price-only competition.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied LMM estimate is 285 firms, but flooring and stock-product firms must be removed before assessing the acquisition pool. Custom millwork is fragmented and often founder-led, supporting succession potential, yet relationships, estimating judgment, and craftsmanship may be concentrated in the owner and reduce transferability.
narrative.demand_durability: Demand follows residential and commercial construction, remodeling, restoration, and design preferences. Medium-term housing forecasts provide support, while current remodeling softness, rates, credit, imports, and substitution to simpler or non-wood finishes create downside. Physical custom output still requires an accountable fabricator even when design and production planning become highly automated.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The chief risks are code heterogeneity, uncertain lens eligibility, project cyclicality, bespoke data, owner dependence, implementation gaps between office and shop, safety, and aggressive builder procurement. The thesis weakens if eligible firms lack repeat customers, drawings cannot be standardized, rework is already low, or project buyers extract all productivity gains through rebidding.
narrative.principal_driver: Automation of takeoff-to-CNC information flow plus yield, rework, and scheduling improvements in repeat custom millwork programs.
narrative.principal_weakness: The code mixes attractive service-like custom fabrication with excluded standardized flooring and product manufacturing, making the true eligible pool uncertain.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: Manufacturing definitions
sources.supports: Defines NAICS 321918 and lists molding, ornamental woodwork, stairwork, flooring, and shutters while excluding windows, doors, and cut stock.

sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024
sources.supports: Shows the broader other-wood-product workforce is concentrated in assemblers, woodworking and sawing machine operators, material movers, supervisors, carpenters, drivers, sales, and managers.

sources.title: Cabinet Design Software and CNC Manufacturing Automation
sources.supports: Documents CAD-based design and integration with CNC machinery for custom cabinetry and commercial millwork production.

sources.title: Custom Woodworking Equipment and Custom Timber Processing Machines
sources.supports: Describes CNC contouring, machine tending, vision-guided sorting, stacking, palletizing, and conveying for custom millwork, while noting irregular geometry makes automation difficult.

sources.title: O*NET: Helpers--Production Workers
sources.supports: Documents physical loading, machine operation, inspection, monitoring, packing, recordkeeping, and material-movement tasks.

sources.title: Only 3.8% of Businesses Use AI to Produce Goods and Services
sources.supports: Reports 3.8% current US business AI use and 6.5% planned use within six months in late 2023, indicating a low adoption base.

sources.title: BLS Table 1: Incidence rates of nonfatal occupational injuries and illnesses by industry, 2024
sources.supports: Reports a total recordable rate of 4.0 cases per 100 full-time workers for NAICS 321918 in 2024.

sources.title: Builders FirstSource 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes custom millwork such as intricate moldings, stair parts, and columns, long-term builder relationships, competitive pricing, service, multiple suppliers, and robust competition.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports 14% of working small-business owners planned a sale, IPO, or transfer within five years, rising to 17% among owners age 55 or older.

sources.title: The Outlook for Housing Starts
sources.supports: Projects housing starts averaging 1.68 million annually in 2025-2029 and 1.52 million in 2030-2033, with financial and demographic uncertainty.

sources.title: Leading Indicator of Remodeling Activity
sources.supports: Projects owner-occupied improvement and maintenance spending growth to slow sharply in early 2027 and states the measure is nominal with a four-quarter horizon.

sources.title: Mohawk Industries 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Reports wood flooring at 12.5% of 2024 US flooring sales but 5.2% of square feet, and documents housing, remodeling, price, import, and substitution pressures.
